talk_title: Regulatory perspective on rare cancer drug development

speaker.name: Martha Donahue
speaker.affiliation: U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Oncology Center of Excellence

MOA_summary: The speaker outlined FDA regulatory incentives, evidentiary standards, and flexible trial design options to accelerate development of therapies for rare cancers, emphasizing that substantial evidence is required regardless of prevalence and that randomized designs are preferred when feasible.

talk_title: Barriers to clinical development of therapeutics for chronic myelomonocytic leukemia

targets.dependency_context: CMML patients with GM-CSF signaling activation

MOA_summary: The presenter highlighted the heterogeneous mutational landscape of CMML, identified convergent GM‑CSF signaling as a therapeutic vulnerability, and described early proof‑of‑concept single‑arm trials targeting JAK2 (ruxolitinib) and GM‑CSF (lenzilumab) that suggest disease‑modifying potential across genetic subsets.
key_findings: CMML incidence is comparable to other myeloproliferative neoplasms that have approved therapies, supporting a viable economic case for drug development.
Single‑arm studies of ruxolitinib (JAK2 inhibitor) and lenzilumab (anti‑GM‑CSF) demonstrated proof‑of‑concept activity in CMML despite small patient numbers.
Phase III randomized trials in Europe (84 patients per arm) completed accrual in 18 months, showing that adequately powered studies are feasible for rare hematologic malignancies.
open_questions: What is the optimal primary endpoint for heterogeneous diseases like CMML—overall survival, transfusion independence, or patient‑reported outcomes?

talk_title: Development of the first FDA‑approved treatment for KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer

speaker.name: Rachel Grisham
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Gynecologic Medical Oncology

targets.dependency_context: KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer (~33% of cases)
Low‑grade serous ovarian cancer; synergizes with MAPK inhibition

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutation
biomarkers.finding: Patients with KRAS‑mutant disease had ORR 44% vs 31% overall and median PFS 22 months

models.name: NGOD‑OV60 GOG 3052 RAMP 201
models.genotype_or_key_features: Recurrent low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, ≥1 prior chemotherapy line, stratified by KRAS status

MOA_summary: Avutametinib, a first‑in‑class RAF/MEK clamp, inhibits MAPK signaling downstream of KRAS, while defactinib blocks focal adhesion kinase; together they produce synergistic tumor regression in KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, achieving >40% response rates and durable disease control.
key_findings: Combination avutametinib + defactinib yielded an overall response rate of 31% in recurrent low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, exceeding the 16‑26% rates seen with prior single‑agent MEK inhibitors.
KRAS‑mutant patients experienced a higher ORR of 44% and a median progression‑free survival of 22 months, establishing KRAS mutation as a predictive biomarker.
The phase 2 RAMP 201 study demonstrated durable responses with a median duration of response >31 months, supporting accelerated approval.
Breakthrough therapy and orphan drug designations facilitated rapid FDA review, leading to accelerated approval within six months of NDA submission.
A seamless phase 2/3 design allowed rapid accrual (>100 patients) and immediate transition to the confirmatory RAMP 301 randomized trial.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Single‑agent avutametinib at a higher dose showed lower efficacy than the combination, indicating that FAK inhibition contributes essential synergistic activity.

combination_strategies.rationale: FAK inhibition synergizes with MAPK blockade to enhance tumor regression and mitigate toxicity of continuous MEK inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rate (31% vs 16‑26% historical) with comparable safety

open_questions: Will the confirmatory RAMP 301 trial demonstrate overall survival benefit sufficient to expand approval to KRAS‑wild‑type patients?
Can the intermittent dosing schedule be optimized further to improve tolerability without compromising efficacy?
